narrative: As a Black male student applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, an LSAT score of 18, a solid Year 1 decile ranking of 7th in your school—above the typical 5.71 average—and a Year 3 decile of 2nd, along with your middle-family-income background and plans to attend full-time. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of past students' paths to the bar exam, predicted a 54% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try, tipping the scales just enough to reject your application since the school prioritizes those likely to pass and succeed. This prediction hinges on a few key factors from your profile that stood out most strongly to the model. The biggest influence pushing toward a failure prediction was your LSAT score, which is notably lower than the average of around 37 seen in the dataset; it signaled to the model a higher risk compared to students with stronger scores who typically pass. On a more positive note, your strong Year 1 decile ranking worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success because it shows early academic promise above what's typical for admitted students. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA, even at 2.70 below the 3.21 average, contributed positively by demonstrating some resilience, though not enough to offset the top concern. Overall, your profile compares to typical students as solid in early performance and background but held back by that standout LSAT gap, which carried the most weight in this close-call decision. While this rejection stings, especially given the extra hurdles Black students like yourself often face in competitive fields like law, it's not the end of your journey. To strengthen future applications, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to or above average, as it could dramatically shift predictions in your favor. You might also consider programs offering LSAT prep tailored for underrepresented students or ways to highlight your Year 1 strengths through recommendations. Keep pushing—many have turned similar profiles around and passed the bar on their first try. Your full-time commitment and middle-income grit are assets that shine through, so build on them strategically for the next round.
